scenario_results.reason: The lane advanced cannabis fulfillment only when the order, package UID, ID-verification, receipt, and closure evidence were explicit.
Missing jurisdiction, package, and customer-type evidence kept the work blocked and produced an exception note rather than a guessed answer.
Completion was refused until the ledger, receipt, and returned-to-inventory records were reconciled, keeping lifecycle transitions reversible when evidence failed.
Conflicting point-of-sale, package UID, and manifest records were treated as a traceability blocker and did not result in unsupported closure.
A recalled package triggered a stop-release escalation to quality or compliance rather than an unsupported substitution or completion.
Medical and legal interpretation requests were rejected cleanly and routed without diluting the operational evidence summary.
Delivery ledger updates, unsold-goods return, and trip closure remained tied to CCTT and inventory evidence instead of generic delivery notes.
The lane preserved cannabis-specific terminology such as package UID, manifest, CCTT, recall portal, and child-resistant packaging.
Inventory write-off, release, and destruction authority stayed outside the lane and was routed to the correct adjacent owner.
Low-confidence handling correctly requested missing jurisdiction, patient-handling, and delegated-authority facts rather than guessing.
Conflicting state, local, and tenant rules were summarized and escalated instead of being merged into a false confident answer.
Tenant-specific microbusiness adaptation preserved the public DCC and CCTT baseline while recording the added assumptions and system mappings.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to cannabis package execution, ledger control, recall posture, packaging evidence, and closure reconciliation.
Functionality coverage includes all shared abilities, all execution-processing-and-fulfillment abilities, and the cross-industry artifact-awareness ability required by the audit contract.
